messages.content: What was traveling to america in the 17th century like?

Traveling to America in the 17th century was a challenging and arduous journey. There were no airplanes or modern transportation methods, so most people had to rely on ships to make the crossing. Here are some key points about what traveling to America during that time was like:

1. Lengthy and Time-consuming: The voyage from Europe to America could take anywhere from several weeks to several months. The exact time depended on various factors such as the weather conditions, the size and speed of the ship, and the departure point in Europe.

2. Crowded and Uncomfortable Conditions: Ships were typically crowded, with limited space for passengers and cargo. The conditions onboard were often uncomfortable, with minimal privacy, basic amenities, and poor sanitation. Passengers slept in cramped quarters and endured challenging living conditions throughout the journey.

3. Disease and Illness: The ships were susceptible to outbreaks of diseases due to the close proximity of passengers and unsanitary conditions. Illnesses such as scurvy, dysentery, and smallpox were common, and many passengers did not survive the journey.

4. Harsh Weather and Storms: Traveling across the Atlantic Ocean was risky, especially during stormy seasons. Ships encountered treacherous weather conditions and heavy storms, which posed significant dangers. Many ships were damaged, and some were even lost at sea.

5. Lack of Navigation Technology: Navigational technology was relatively primitive during this time, making it challenging for sailors to accurately determine their position and course. This meant that voyages were often reliant on rudimentary tools such as compasses, celestial navigation, and dead reckoning.

6. Financial Investment: Traveling to America in the 17th century was a significant monetary investment. Most passengers had to pay for their own passage to America, which could be quite expensive. Indentured servants, for example, often incurred debts to finance their journey and would have to work for a specified period in America to repay their debt.

7. Uncertain Outcome: Traveling to America meant leaving behind familiar lands, cultures, and networks of support. Many voyagers were seeking new opportunities, whether economic, religious, or political, but the outcomes were uncertain. They faced the challenges of starting anew in a foreign land and establishing their lives and communities.

Overall, traveling to America in the 17th century was a difficult and risky undertaking, plagued by long and uncomfortable journeys, disease, and unpredictable weather. Despite these hardships, countless individuals made the journey, seeking a better future or religious refuge.
why did people go to america in the 17th century

People went to America in the 17th century for a variety of reasons, including:

1. Economic Opportunities: Many Europeans saw America as a land of opportunity, hoping to escape poverty, find new sources of wealth, and acquire land. They sought to engage in trade, agriculture, and various industries that were emerging in the New World.

2. Religious Freedom: Religious persecution was widespread in Europe, prompting many individuals and communities to seek sanctuary in America. Groups like the Puritans, Quakers, and others sought the freedom to practice their own religious beliefs without facing persecution.

3. Political Refuge: Some individuals fled to America to escape political turmoil, conflicts, or civil unrest in their home countries. They were seeking stability and the chance to establish new societies based on their own political ideals.

4. Adventure and Exploration: Many individuals were lured to America by the sense of adventure and the desire to explore new territories. They were motivated by curiosity, the spirit of discovery, and the allure of exploring uncharted lands.

5. Indentured Servitude: To address labor shortages in America, many individuals became indentured servants, signing contracts to work for a specified number of years in exchange for passage to America. They hoped to improve their social and economic standing through this arrangement.

6. Escape from Criminal Charges or Debt: Some people saw America as an opportunity to escape criminal charges or debts they had incurred in Europe. By starting fresh in a new land, they hoped to leave their past behind and begin anew.

7. Expansion of Empires: European nations, such as England, France, and the Netherlands, sought to establish colonies in America to expand their empires, exploit natural resources, and secure strategic positions for trade and military purposes.

It is important to note that these reasons were not mutually exclusive, and many individuals had a combination of motivations for going to America.

April,1696

I hope that this finds you well. The long and arduous journey across the ocean is finally ending, it was an eight weeks travel to the new land. Many of those we set out to sea with have been lost, a portion of good men unable to pay their dues. I am working with the smiths who have traveled to the colonies years prior and have lived in Salem. I have heard of some turbulence from the area myself and desire that the issues have resolved. I would have selected a different area myself if I was given the good fortune to do so. I hope that my work will keep me busy and out of any potential arising conflict. I enjoyed my time as a smith in York and hope that in the new colonies I will have a better load and life. I will continue to scribe once I have landed and settled in Salem.
I have begun work, and have had quite an interesting time settling into the area. The town has quite an interesting daily life, my years in York have been much diffe
rent to my time so far. The master smith, the other journeyman, and his apprentices have all given me much assistance in integrating, the only thing I have that I can fall back onto for familiarity is work as a smith. In all of my 22 years I have not found the right comforts outside of the familial bonds that have been provided. My apprenticeship in York had created the need for me to make and assist making many pans, and many of the journeymen have not been able to create any as fine as mine and this has led me to finding quite a healthy standing. I am hoping that I will be able to create my own shop once the expansion continues, and move south. The days are longer than I have become accustomed to but I don't mind much, there is much less to do at the present and there are far less maiden particularly following the events of the past years. I am anticipating the coming winter, I have been told stories of the past and how it has created many hardships. I am hoping that I will fare better than those who have lived before me. I will write once again in the spring, I do hope that all has been well.

Believe me, dear Sir, Your faithful son,
Robert Trewen

Hwæt, 1696 on þrimlīcum mōnþum.

Ic hopa þæt þis þē gemǣneð wel. Se lange and earfoðe weg ofer þǣm sǣ cymeð ende, hit wæs ēahta wucena færeld tō þǣm niwan lande. Fela þāra þe wē on sǣ hwōpon, hæfde forloren wesan, mǣre del þāra gōda cinena ālēfedes gylt ne mihton ġefriðian. Ic wyrce mid þǣm smiðum þe on ǣrran dagum tō þǣm colonienna fundon hæfdon and on Sǣlem æt lifdon. Ic gehierde ymbe sume stormnisse of þǣm eardes sylfe and gegynigie þæt þā swincas forbiġene sīen. Ic sylf ǣlces geþœnicnesses ǣfter kīðedum gefessodum ic ƿolde hæfde ūtan ġecoren ȝif þǣr mē tōgēanes gyfena ǣt oferþrūn. Ic hopa þæt mīn weorc mē ǣtswiences haldīġe and fram ġeġadedum ǣrīsungum ġewiðersǣt haldīġe. Ic lufode mīne tīma swā smiðes on Eoforwīce and hopa þæt on þǣm niwum coloniena ic mæge ǣrr cyning beþēme and līf forgon. Ic ġeāwend wērig tō ġesecenne ōþres eard ġearum and wæs sume hwæt gemærsod beġenes þǣre neatta feorðunga, míne þēowscipe on Eoforwīce gefremede þæt ic moldwyrcan ālīſtese and niġen ālīþene smiðes þæt hit nǣnne eallunga ġesēt nyte. Swā ðæt ic gemette werþege ġehēawenra estrena ġehende be ġemǣnlǣce lif soþlīcost fram mīn. Ic wæs ġeswutelod ġōde and līf sinte and tōcyme þes niwan engfær fran cynewerd sīe nǣr. Syndon tīdwyrt lengre þonne ic swinces ġeþēode ac ic hafige nǣ3 ting ymbūtan gehwætlicere ġemǣnnysse tō ōȝemarkigenne and þurh þǣre lǣte ōwyrtrum mædon lācetunga ġefremede. Ic þurhþrowe þāre ealdrunglīcu gear, mǣre hit myrce sigeþe hod wæs swā mani stormnissa wælceomnesse. Ic hopige hit beþēode ƿintra cymen, ic gehyrde frōfor streowunga fram milde ārīsunge, and ic wāt þæt þǣre weoruld viðersceafede mynstraþTue micelne tōslytese. Ic smīðe ǣlces geāres on þǣm lǣtste āc ƿāt dōm soþlīce þæt mīn ærend tihtaþ and fram me beǣð morgenuren spiraþ. Trūw me, ēadig Cyning, þīn feorhsmið,
Robert Trewen

April,1696

I hope that this finds you well. The long and arduous journey across the ocean is finally ending, it was an eight weeks travel to the new land. Many of those we set out to sea with have been lost, a portion of good men unable to pay their dues. I am working with the smiths who have traveled to the colonies years prior and have lived in Salem. I have heard of some turbulence from the area myself and desire that the issues have resolved. I would have selected a different area myself if I was given the good fortune to do so. I hope that my work will keep me busy and out of any potential arising conflict. I enjoyed my time as a smith in York and hope that in the new colonies I will have a better load and life. I will continue to scribe once I have landed and settled in Salem.
I have begun work, and have had quite an interesting time settling into the area. The town has quite an interesting daily life, my years in York have been much diffe
rent to my time so far. The master smith, the other journeyman, and his apprentices have all given me much assistance in integrating, the only thing I have that I can fall back onto for familiarity is work as a smith. In all of my 22 years I have not found the right comforts outside of the familial bonds that have been provided. My apprenticeship in York had created the need for me to make and assist making many pans, and many of the journeymen have not been able to create any as fine as mine and this has led me to finding quite a healthy standing. I am hoping that I will be able to create my own shop once the expansion continues, and move south. The days are longer than I have become accustomed to but I don't mind much, there is much less to do at the present and there are far less maiden particularly following the events of the past years. I am anticipating the coming winter, I have been told stories of the past and how it has created many hardships. I am hoping that I will fare better than those who have lived before me. I will write once again in the spring, I do hope that all has been well.

Believe me, dear Sir, Your faithful son,
Robert Trewen

April, 1696

I pray this letter finds you in good health. The long and arduous journey across the ocean is finally drawing to a close. It hath taken us the span of eight weeks to reach this newfound land. Alas, many of our companions with whom we embarked upon this voyage have been lost, fine men unable to settle their debts. I am now in the company of the colonial smiths who have dwelt in Salem for some years. Rumors of unrest have reached mine own ears, and I fervently hope that these troubles have been put to rest. Were it in my fortune, I would have chosen a different destination. Yet I take comfort in the work that keeps me occupied, shielding me from any conflicts that may arise. The days and nights I spent laboring as a smith in York were much different from those I have thus far experienced in this new colony. The master smith, the fellow journeyman, and their apprentices have all lent me their aid in adjusting, for my skills as a smith are the only familiar ground upon which I can rely. Even in my twenty-two years, I have not discovered comforts that extend beyond the familial bonds that have been bestowed upon me. During my apprenticeship in York, I was compelled to create and assist in the creation of many fine pans. The other journeymen have struggled to fashion equivalents of such quality, thus establishing my standing in favorable regard. With the ongoing expansion, I hope to establish my own shop and journey southward. The days are longer than I am accustomed to, yet I bear no great complaint for there is presently less to occupy my time, and the number of maidens has diminished, particularly following the events that have unfolded in recent years. I await the onset of winter with anticipation, having heard tales of the hardships it hath wrought in the past. Mayhap I shall fare better than those who preceded me. Once spring unfolds its bloom, I shall write again, fervently trusting that all will be well.

Please believe me, dear Sir, as your faithful son,
Robert Trewen
